content.text: Why OSS Governance Is More Complex for GenAI

Open-source software governance in GenAI applications must address two distinct layers that do not exist in traditional software: the model layer and the framework/tooling layer. Both carry licence obligations, and the rules that govern them differ in ways that catch teams off guard.

On the framework side, the established OSS licence categories — permissive, weak copyleft, strong copyleft — apply as they do elsewhere, but the AGPL clause that extends copyleft to network-served software is particularly significant for GenAI APIs. On the model side, most of the widely-used open-weight models (Llama, Mistral, Falcon) use custom community licences that are not OSI-approved and contain restrictions — on commercial use, user count, or competing AI services — that teams routinely overlook.

'Open source' in the model context often means 'weights are publicly downloadable', not 'the licence is OSI-approved and permits unrestricted commercial use'. Read the actual licence text before deploying any open-weight model in a production product.

attrs.alt: OSS licence risk tiers for GenAI components: Tier 1 permissive, Tier 2 weak copyleft, Tier 3 strong copyleft

content.text: Permissive licences impose minimal obligations: retain the copyright notice, and in the case of Apache 2.0, preserve any NOTICE file. They do not require disclosure of proprietary modifications or source code. The vast majority of the Python ML ecosystem — PyTorch, TensorFlow, Hugging Face Transformers, LangChain, LlamaIndex — uses Apache 2.0 or MIT. For GenAI application development, permissive-licensed dependencies are the safe default.
Apache 2.0 adds one valuable clause not present in MIT: an explicit patent licence from each contributor. This is material for AI tooling where patent risk is real.

Weak copyleft licences require that modifications to the licensed component itself are released under the same licence, but allow proprietary code to use the component as a library without triggering copyleft on the proprietary code. The critical distinction is linking:
Dynamic linking (importing an LGPL library at runtime via a shared library or package manager) is generally safe for proprietary products.
Static linking (embedding the LGPL library's code directly into the compiled binary) typically requires the modified library source to be disclosed.

For Python and JavaScript — interpreted languages where all dependencies are effectively dynamically loaded — LGPL is generally safe, but consult Legal before using LGPL components in any product shipped as compiled binaries.

Strong copyleft licences require that any work that incorporates the licensed code also be released under the same licence. For commercial proprietary products, this is typically a showstopper. The three variants that appear most frequently in AI contexts:
Any software distributed that links to or incorporates GPL code must be GPL
Rarely appears in core ML frameworks; check older scientific code
Extends GPL copyleft to network use — serving software over a network counts as distribution
Any AI service using AGPL-licensed inference code must open-source the entire service codebase
Copyleft extends to any software providing the licensed software as a service
Rare in ML but check database and storage components used by RAG pipelines

The AGPL clause is the most consequential for GenAI: if your inference stack or any component of your model-serving pipeline uses AGPL-licensed code, serving predictions over an API endpoint technically constitutes distribution, requiring the entire application source to be released. Some ML libraries do carry AGPL licences — audit your dependency tree before assuming safety.
Model weight licences are not software licences and are not OSI-approved. They are contractual terms set by the model creator. The key dimensions to check for any open-weight model:
Some models prohibit commercial use entirely or require a commercial licence above a revenue threshold
Is there a monthly active user (MAU) cap below which the community licence applies?
Llama 3: community licence up to 700M MAU; above requires Meta licence agreement
May the model be used to build a product that competes with the model creator?
Several model licences prohibit using the model to train competing models or offer competing API services
Are there restrictions on outputs that can be generated?
Acceptable use policies prohibit generating harmful content; some restrict specific use cases
Can the weights be redistributed or hosted?
Fine-tuned weights may need to carry the original licence; hosting for third parties may be restricted
The following covers the models most commonly deployed in enterprise GenAI applications as of mid-2025:
Llama 3 (Meta): Community licence permits commercial use for organisations with fewer than 700M monthly active users. Competing AI service use is restricted. Meta must be named in derived products.
Mistral (Mistral AI): Apache 2.0 for base models — permissive and commercially safe. Check individual fine-tuned variants as they may carry different terms.
Falcon (TII): Apache 2.0 — permissive. The original Falcon licence was more restrictive; Apache 2.0 now applies.
Stable Diffusion (Stability AI): CreativeML Open RAIL-M — prohibits use to generate content that violates applicable law or to mislead users about AI-generated nature.
GPT-2, GPT-Neo (EleutherAI): MIT — permissive. GPT-J uses Apache 2.0.
OSS Usage Policy for GenAI Applications

The OSS usage policy applies to all software components used in GenAI applications, including: ML frameworks, inference libraries, vector databases, prompt management tools, evaluation frameworks, and model weights. The policy does not apply to internal tooling that is never shipped to production.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class ApprovalTier(str, Enum):
    PRE_APPROVED = "pre_approved"      # use without review
    LEGAL_REVIEW = "legal_review"      # review before first use
    PROHIBITED = "prohibited"          # do not use without commercial licence

@dataclass
class OSSComponent:
    name: str
    version: str
    licence: str
    component_type: str   # "framework", "model_weights", "database", "tool"
    approval_tier: ApprovalTier
    notes: str
    approved_by: Optional[str] = None
    approved_date: Optional[str] = None
    
    def can_use_immediately(self) -> bool:
        return self.approval_tier == ApprovalTier.PRE_APPROVED
    
    def requires_legal(self) -> bool:
        return self.approval_tier == ApprovalTier.LEGAL_REVIEW
    
    def is_blocked(self) -> bool:
        return self.approval_tier == ApprovalTier.PROHIBITED

# Pre-approved — Tier 1 licences and known safe models
PRE_APPROVED = [
    OSSComponent("pytorch", "2.x", "BSD-3-Clause", "framework", ApprovalTier.PRE_APPROVED, ""),
    OSSComponent("transformers", "4.x", "Apache-2.0", "framework", ApprovalTier.PRE_APPROVED, ""),
    OSSComponent("langchain", "0.x", "MIT", "framework", ApprovalTier.PRE_APPROVED, ""),
    OSSComponent("llama-index", "0.x", "MIT", "framework", ApprovalTier.PRE_APPROVED, ""),
    OSSComponent("mistral-7b-v0.3", "0.3", "Apache-2.0", "model_weights", ApprovalTier.PRE_APPROVED, ""),
]

# Requires legal review — Tier 2 and custom model licences
LEGAL_REVIEW = [
    OSSComponent("llama-3-70b", "3.0", "Llama 3 Community", "model_weights", ApprovalTier.LEGAL_REVIEW,
                 "MAU limit 700M; competing service restricted"),
    OSSComponent("some-lgpl-lib", "1.0", "LGPL-3.0", "framework", ApprovalTier.LEGAL_REVIEW,
                 "Check static vs dynamic linking"),
]

# Prohibited without commercial licence
PROHIBITED = [
    OSSComponent("some-agpl-inference", "1.0", "AGPL-3.0", "framework", ApprovalTier.PROHIBITED,
                 "Network service use triggers copyleft on entire codebase"),
    OSSComponent("some-gpl-tool", "1.0", "GPL-3.0", "tool", ApprovalTier.PROHIBITED,
                 "Copyleft incompatible with proprietary product"),
]
Manual licence tracking is insufficient for codebases with hundreds of dependencies. The policy requires automated licence scanning integrated into the CI pipeline. Recommended tools:
FOSSA or Snyk for continuous licence scanning of Python, JavaScript, and Go dependency trees.
scancode-toolkit for deeper analysis of embedded code and non-standard licences.
pip-licenses (Python) or license-checker (Node.js) for quick local checks during development.

# CI licence scan — fail build if prohibited licences detected
pip install pip-licenses

pip-licenses --format=json --output-file=licence-report.json

python3 - << 'EOF'
import json, sys

PROHIBITED = {"GPL-2.0", "GPL-3.0", "AGPL-3.0", "SSPL-1.0"}
REVIEW_REQUIRED = {"LGPL-2.1", "LGPL-3.0", "MPL-2.0", "EUPL-1.2"}

with open("licence-report.json") as f:
    packages = json.load(f)

blocked = [p for p in packages if p["License"] in PROHIBITED]
review = [p for p in packages if p["License"] in REVIEW_REQUIRED]

if blocked:
    print("BUILD FAILED — prohibited licences detected:")
    for p in blocked:
        print(f"  {p['Name']} {p['Version']}: {p['License']}")
    sys.exit(1)

if review:
    print("WARNING — licences requiring legal review:")
    for p in review:
        print(f"  {p['Name']} {p['Version']}: {p['License']}")
    # Warning only — does not fail build; legal team reviews

print(f"Licence scan passed: {len(packages)} packages, {len(review)} for review.")
EOF

A Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) is a machine-readable manifest of every component — library, model weight, dataset — included in a deployed artefact. For GenAI applications an SBOM must capture two distinct layers: the
(pip packages, npm modules, system libraries) and the
(model provider, base model lineage, fine-tuning datasets if known).

Regulatory frameworks are moving toward mandatory SBOM publication. The US Executive Order 14028 requires SBOM for software sold to US federal agencies. ENISA's draft AI Security Guidelines (2024) extend this to AI models. Proactively maintaining an SBOM positions your team for compliance with minimal last-minute scramble.

# Generate CycloneDX SBOM for Python dependencies
pip install cyclonedx-bom
cyclonedx-bom --format json --output sbom-python.json

# Generate for Node.js
npx @cyclonedx/cyclonedx-npm --output-format JSON --output-file sbom-node.json

# Merge SBOMs and run licence gate (custom script)
python scripts/merge_sboms.py sbom-python.json sbom-node.json > sbom-merged.json
python scripts/licence_gate.py sbom-merged.json --fail-on PROHIBITED --warn-on LEGAL_REVIEW

# Upload to SBOM tracker (example: Dependency-Track)
curl -X POST https://dtrack.internal/api/v1/bom \
  -H "X-Api-Key: $DTRACK_API_KEY" \
  -F "project=$PROJECT_UUID" \
  -F "bom=@sbom-merged.json"
When combining multiple OSS components in a single artefact, the
most restrictive licence governs the combined work

. The matrix below summarises compatibility for the licence combinations most common in GenAI stacks. A tick (✓) means the combination is distributable without additional obligations; a cross (✗) means distribution requires legal review or is blocked.

The AGPL-3.0 'SaaS trigger' means that serving an AGPL library over a network API requires publishing your full application source. This is particularly relevant for GenAI inference APIs — even wrapping an AGPL model-serving library can trigger disclosure obligations.

Link AGPL and GPL libraries only if your application is itself open-sourced under the same terms. Any proprietary GenAI product must use permissively licensed or commercially licensed inference runtimes.

Engineers frequently want to contribute bug fixes or improvements back to upstream OSS projects, including model libraries, tokenisers, and inference servers. An OSS contribution policy must address IP assignment, CLA requirements, export controls, and competitive sensitivity.
Submitting PRs to repositories that use a Contributor Licence Agreement (CLA) — the CLA must be reviewed by Legal before signing.
Contributing code that derives from internal proprietary algorithms, training recipes, or prompt engineering techniques, even if the contribution appears superficial.
Open-sourcing datasets or fine-tuning configurations — these may contain GDPR-regulated derived signals or commercially sensitive labelling schemes.
Contributions to repositories that implement functionality your organisation considers a competitive differentiator.
Documentation fixes, typo corrections, and comment improvements with no functional change.
Bug fixes in third-party libraries that are purely defensive (fix a crash, fix a memory leak) with no novel algorithm or business logic.
Test cases that verify publicly documented API contracts — no internal data or prompt strategies included.
Infrastructure improvements (CI configuration, dependency updates) to projects where you are already a listed maintainer under an approved CLA.

An OSS usage policy for GenAI applications must address two distinct licence layers — standard software frameworks and libraries, and model weight licences — because they carry different obligations and the standard licence categories do not map cleanly to model licences.
Tier 1 permissive licences (MIT, Apache 2.0, BSD) are the safe default for frameworks; retain copyright notices and NOTICE files.
Tier 2 weak copyleft licences (LGPL, MPL 2.0) require legal review before use, especially when static linking or compiled distribution is involved.
Tier 3 strong copyleft licences (GPL, AGPL, SSPL) are prohibited in proprietary products without a commercial licence; AGPL is especially dangerous for API services.
Model weight licences are not OSI-approved — check commercial use permission, MAU limits, competing service restrictions, and output use policies for every model used in production.
Automate licence scanning in CI with tools like FOSSA, Snyk, or pip-licenses; block builds that introduce prohibited licences.
Maintain an approved component registry with pre-approved, legal-review, and prohibited tiers; require legal sign-off before any Tier 2 or novel model licence component enters production.
